sections.heading: Introduction and MAPK Pathway
sections.content: Cutaneous melanoma is driven by aberrant activation of signaling pathways controlling cell proliferation, survival, and differentiation. The mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) pathway is the most commonly dysregulated pathway in cutaneous melanoma [146-148].

sections.heading: BRAF Mutations
sections.content: BRAF mutations, occurring in approximately 40%-50% of cutaneous melanomas, are the most frequent driver alterations. BRAF encodes a serine/threonine kinase that activates the MAPK pathway; activating mutations at codon 600 lead to constitutive kinase activity and unrestrained cell growth [146,44]. BRAF V600E accounts for approximately 80% of BRAF mutations, V600K for approximately 15%, and V600R/M/D/G for approximately 5% [44,ME-C2]. BRAF mutations are more common in melanomas arising on intermittently sun-exposed skin, in younger patients, and with truncal location [43]. BRAF mutations outside codon 600, including non-V600 mutations and BRAF gene fusions, occur in approximately 5% of melanomas [ME-C2]. BRAF gene fusion-positive tumors have shown responses to MEK inhibitors and non-specific RAF inhibitors [ME-C2].

sections.heading: NRAS Mutations
sections.content: NRAS mutations, encoding a GTPase that activates MAPK and other signaling pathways, are present in approximately 15% of melanomas across all sun-exposure patterns and anatomic sites [43, ME-C2]. NRAS mutations correlate with poor survival in both localized and advanced disease [60].

sections.heading: KIT Mutations
sections.content: KIT (proto-oncogene c-KIT) encodes a receptor tyrosine kinase; activating mutations are present in approximately 10%-15% of melanomas arising on mucosal surfaces (vulvovaginal, anorectal, sinonasal) and acral sites, and in 2%-3% of chronically sun-exposed skin melanomas, but are extremely rare in melanomas arising on intermittently sun-exposed skin [38,54, ME-C2]. KIT mutations across multiple hotspots show differential sensitivity to KIT inhibitors; exon 11 and 13 mutations (e.g., W557R, V559D, L576P, K642E) demonstrate high sensitivity to imatinib, while exon 17 mutations (e.g., D816H) show minimal sensitivity [55-58, ME-C2].

sections.heading: Other Genomic Drivers
sections.content: Other uncommon genomic drivers detectable by multigene panel testing (MGPT) include gene fusions in NTRK1/2/3, ALK, and ROS1, each occurring in less than 1% of melanomas across subtypes [ME-C2].

sections.heading: Germline Predisposition
sections.content: Germline predisposition genes associated with familial melanoma include CDKN2A, CDK4, MC1R, BAP1 (especially for uveal melanoma), TERT, POT1, and MITF; additional cancer predisposition genes with increased melanoma risk include TP53, PTEN, CHEK2, BRCA1/2, BLM, and ATM [6,15-17,66,67, ME-A1]. PTEN loss of function is associated with melanoma pathogenesis [ME-A1].

sections.heading: Tumor Mutational Burden
sections.content: Tumor mutational burden (TMB) appears to correlate with response to immune checkpoint inhibitors, with higher mutation burden potentially increasing neoantigen production and immunogenicity [72-74, ME-C4].

sections.heading: Circulating Tumor DNA
sections.content: Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) has shown potential utility in melanoma; detectable ctDNA following surgical resection of stage III/IV melanoma correlates with worse prognosis in adjuvant studies, though most patients have no detectable ctDNA postoperatively [ME-C3].
